coredata.dc:title: Synthesis, characterization, and investigation of antibacterial activity of Novel CMC/CuO NPs/CQDs bionanocomposite coating
coredata.dc:creator: Amoon, Hamidreza
Moghadam, Ayoub
Hajkarim, Maryam Chalabi
coredata.dc:description: In recent decades, healthcare-associated infections (HAIs) have become a common problem in healthcare facilities such as hospitals. As a result, researchers are currently developing nanocomposite coatings that are strengthened with antibacterial nanoparticles. In this research, a novel antibacterial bionanocomposite coating based on carboxymethyl cellulose polymer/copper oxide nanoparticles/carbon quantum dots was coated on medical grade 316 stainless steel by sol-gel dip-coating method. The effect of the concentration of nanocomposite components was investigated at four different levels to determine the best ratio with the most antibacterial activity. Structural characteristics of nanocomposite and coating were investigated using different analysis methods. The coating analysis showed that reinforcements are uniformly distributed in the polymer matrix. Antibacterial test of disc diffusion was performed by the Kirby-Bauer method and minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) antibacterial test. The results showed that bionanocomposite was effective in the MIC assays against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli with MIC values of 25 mg/ml and >50 mg/ml, respectively. The inhibition zones for E. coli and S. aureus were 17 and 32 mm, respectively, at 10 μg/disc of gentamicin. SEM images displayed significant and evident alterations in the structure of bacterial morphology, indicating cellular damage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CuO nanoparticles
Carbon quantum dots
Carboxymethyl cellulose
Antimicrobial activity
Sol-gel dipping
Nanocomposite

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Minimum inhibitory concentration and minimum bactericidal concentration determination
Study of changes of bacterial morphology
FESEM micrograph and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy
The morphology changes of the bacteria

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and (b) size distribution histogram of CQDs.
X-ray diffraction pattern of nanocomposite and its components.
The IR spectrum of nanocomposites and their components.
UV–Vis spectroscopy of nanocomposite and its components.
(a and b) The FESEM images in different magnifications, (c) size distribution histogram, and (d) X-ray energy diffraction spectroscopy of nanocomposite.
FESEM micrograph: (a, b, and c) secondary electron images (d) backscattered electron image, and (e and f) cross section images of the nanocomposite coating.
Linear X-ray energy diffraction analysis of nanocomposite coating.
Elemental mapping analysis of nanocomposite (top row) and coating (bottom row).
Disk diffusion assay. (A) Antibacterial effect of CMC/CuO NPs/CQDs 2.5 and 10 mg/ml on E. coli. (B) Antibacterial effect of CMC/ CuO NPs/CQDs 2.5 and 10 mg/ml on S. aureus. GEN: Gentamicin (10 μg).

MIC and MBC assay. Antibacterial effect of CMC/CuO NPs/CQDs against S. aureus. (A) MBC value: 50 mg/ml. (B) MIC value: 25 mg/ml. (C) CMC/CuO NPs/CQDs 12.5 mg/ml. (D) CMC/CuO NPs/CQDs 6.25 mg/ml. (E) CMC/CuO NPs/CQDs 3.13 mg/ml. (F) C+: CMC/CuO NPs/CQDs 0 mg/ml.

MIC and MBC assay. Antibacterial effect of CMC/CuO NPs/CQDs against E. coli. (A) MBC value: >50 mg/ml. (B) CMC/CuO NPs/CQDs 25 mg/ml. (C) CMC/CuO NPs/CQDs 12.5 mg/ml. (D) CMC/CuO NPs/CQDs 6.25 mg/ml. (E) CMC/CuO NPs/CQDs 3.13 mg/ml. (F) C+: CMC/CuO NPs/CQDs 0 mg/ml.
Concentration levels of raw materials based on Taguchi's design.
Weight percentage of elements in nanocomposite coating based on linear energy X-ray diffraction analysis.

head.ce:title: Synthesis, characterization, and investigation of antibacterial activity of Novel CMC/CuO NPs/CQDs bionanocomposite coating

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of materials science and engineering, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Medical Biology Research Center, Health Technology Institute, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Medical Biology Research Center, Health Technology Institute, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent decades, healthcare-associated infections (HAIs) have become a common problem in healthcare facilities such as hospitals. As a result, researchers are currently developing nanocomposite coatings that are strengthened with antibacterial nanoparticles. In this research, a novel antibacterial bionanocomposite coating based on carboxymethyl cellulose polymer/copper oxide nanoparticles/carbon quantum dots was coated on medical grade 316 stainless steel by sol-gel dip-coating method. The effect of the concentration of nanocomposite components was investigated at four different levels to determine the best ratio with the most antibacterial activity. Structural characteristics of nanocomposite and coating were investigated using different analysis methods. The coating analysis showed that reinforcements are uniformly distributed in the polymer matrix. Antibacterial test of disc diffusion was performed by the Kirby-Bauer method and minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) antibacterial test. The results showed that bionanocomposite was effective in the MIC assays against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli with MIC values of 25 mg/ml and >50 mg/ml, respectively. The inhibition zones for E. coli and S. aureus were 17 and 32 mm, respectively, at 10 μg/disc of gentamicin. SEM images displayed significant and evident alterations in the structure of bacterial morphology, indicating cellular damage.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Healthcare-associated infections are infections that people may acquire while receiving medical healthcare for another condition. These infections can occur in any healthcare facility in hospitals. In recent times, efforts to prevent of HAIs have shifted towards understanding the physical environment's role in transmitting pathogens. The developing technology in the field of antimicrobial coating can have an important contribution in overcoming biofilms in various fields such as food, medicine, medical equipment, clothing industry and agriculture [1–3]. In recent years, there has been ongoing research and development of new antimicrobial coatings on the surface of various objects such as medical devices [4].

Metal ion-based nanomaterials demonstrate a wide-ranging antibacterial effect and effectively combat bacteria, fungi, and viruses. However, composite materials lack antimicrobial properties, leading to several attempts to incorporate materials with antimicrobial activity into composites. In this regard, one of the suggested materials is nanoscale antimicrobial particles like copper oxide nanoparticles, which can be combined with the composite [5]. Metal and metal oxide nanoparticles possess numerous applications in antibacterial activities owing to their exceptionally high surface-to-volume ratio [6].

Research findings have demonstrated that when the size of nanoparticles decreases, their properties and behaviors undergo a transformation, leading to the emergence of unique and innovative attributes. These benefits have contributed to the rapid acceleration in the utilization of nanomaterials [7,8]. Many factors including the formation of bacterial colonies, cell growth of bacteria, and the formation of microbial biofilm can cause infection and make bacteria resistant to the host's defense system. Therefore, nanoparticles can remove this bacterial resistance [9].

Copper-based nanoparticles are highly regarded among metal nanoparticles because of their affordability, accessibility, and comparable properties to other metal nanoparticles [10]. Copper oxide nanoparticles exhibit efficacy not only by generating reactive oxygen species but also by inhibiting the intracellular antioxidants of bacteria [11,12]. Previous research observed that copper oxide nanoparticles have biocidal activity because they are effective against different viruses [13–15].

Carboxymethyl cellulose is a commonly utilized cellulose derivative known for its excellent chemical stability and non-toxic properties. Despite these advantages, it lacks sufficient antimicrobial activity and mechanical strength to be used independently in antibacterial applications. Today, the CMC is mostly combined with metal and metal oxide nanoparticles, and it significantly improves its mechanical properties and antibacterial activity [16,17].

Carbon nanomaterials like carbon nanotubes, graphene sheets, fullerenes, and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been extensively researched for their potential applications across various industries. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a novel type of nanomaterials ranging from 1 to 10 nm in size, are particularly intriguing to industries due to their exceptional fluorescence, water solubility, biocompatibility, and antimicrobial characteristics. Bactericide via CQDs is typically achieved through ROS-induced oxidative stress, often necessitating the use of H2O2 or photostimulation [18–20].

The 316L stainless steel, known for its low carbon content (L), is an ideal material for the production of medical equipment and surgical instruments. This steel possesses excellent qualities including corrosion resistance, mechanical strength, biocompatibility, high temperature resistance, and non-reactive properties. Therefore, it is highly suitable for the manufacturing of dental extraction forceps, kidney dishes, thoracic retractors, ambulatory surgical centers, bed rails, overbed tables, and IV poles are often contaminated with harmful microorganisms [21,22].

In this study, polymeric bionanocomposite with a new combination of carboxymethyl cellulose polymer reinforced with copper oxide nanoparticles and quantum dot carbon was synthesized, and was coated on medical L316 stainless steel alloy by sol-gel dipping process. Structural and antibacterial properties of nanobiocomposite and coating were investigated using different analyzes and antibacterial tests. Also, in this work, it was tried to determine the optimal ratio of concentration of bionanocomposite components to achieve maximum antibacterial properties. The results show that this new antibacterial coating can be a suitable option for use in various medical and non-medical devices as well as surgical and hospital equipment.

Copper oxide nanoparticles with a size of 50 nm were purchased from USA-US. Citric acid and carboxymethyl cellulose was obtained from Merck. Ethylenediamine with analytical grade was acquired from Aldrich. Mueller Hinton Agar and Mueller Hinton Broth were prepared by QUELAB Company. Deionized water was used in all stages.

The synthesis method reported by Jiang et al. was used to produce carbon quantum dots [23]. To synthesize of CQDs, firstly, 1.5 g of ethylenediamine and 1.24 g of citric acid were dissolved in 35 ml of distilled water. Then, the obtained solution was placed in an autoclave and heated for 6 h at 180 °C. Then, naturally, the reactor was cooled to room temperature after the reaction. The obtained transparent and brown-yellow product was separated by a centrifuge and purified by dialysis. Finally, the brownish black solid was obtained through vacuum distillation and drying.

To determine the optimal concentrations for the synthesis of nanocomposite, the Taguchi design was used according to Table 1 . The synthesis method reported by Moghadam et al. was used to produce nanocomposite [24]. The solutions of carboxymethyl cellulose and copper oxide nanoparticles were prepared separately. To achieve this objective, 0.4 g of copper nanoparticles and 0.06 g of carboxymethyl cellulose were dissolved individually in 40 ml of distilled water. Additionally, 0.1 g of quantum carbon dot was dissolved in 20 ml of distilled water. To properly disperse the nanoparticles, the solutions were stirred on a stirrer for 2 h. Then the solution of copper oxide nanoparticles was added dropwise to the carboxymethyl solution on the stirrer and the resulting solution was ultrasonicated for 20 min. Then, the carbon quantum dot solution was added dropwise to the two-component solution of carboxymethyl cellulose - copper oxide nanoparticles, and finally, the final nanocomposite solution was placed on the stirrer for 2 h and was ultrasonicated for 20 min. The ultimate resolution was placed inside an oven at a temperature of 80 °C for 48 h. Finally, the dried nanocomposite was powdered with a mortar.

A piece of 316 stainless steel was cut with the dimensions of 20 × 20 × 2 mm using a cutting machine. Then they were sanded using 80, 320, 400, 600, 800, 1200, 2000, and 3000 silicon carbide sandpapers respectively. Next, they were polished using alumina powder with a size of 1.6 μm so that the surface of the sample became completely mirror-like. Finally, the samples were degreased using acetone in an ultrasonic bath for 11 min, washed with distilled water, and finally dried. Dip-coating solution was prepared according to level 4 of Table 1 and Section 2.3. Next, the substrate was immersed in the solution at a speed of 5 cm/min and remained inside for 6 min. Then the substrate was pulled out of the solution at the mentioned speed and kept at room temperature for 8 h. Then, to dry coating, the coated substrate was placed in an oven for 24 h at a temperature of 80 °C. The above steps were repeated 2 times.

The structure of synthesized nanocomposites and their components were investigated using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Thermo Company, AVATAR model) in the range of 4000 to 400 cm−1. The X-ray diffraction analysis (Philips, pw1730 model) was employed to investigate the crystal structure of the samples. The range of 2Ө was set between 10–80°. To carry out this analysis, a copper cathode lamp with a wavelength of 1.54056 °A was used, along with a voltage of 40 kV and a current of 30 amps. The microstructure of carbon quantum dots was investigated using a high-resolution field emission scanning electron microscope (TEM, TESCAN Company, MIRA3 model) with a working voltage of 50 kV. The X-ray surface elemental mapping test (EDS, x-ray Map, SAMX Company) was utilized to acquire the elemental distribution of components and the dispersion map of the elements in the nanocomposite and coating. Additionally, the X-ray energy diffraction spectroscopy (EDS, Broker model) was employed to obtain the size distribution histogram and identify the constituent elements and their quantities in the nanocomposites. The morphology and size distribution of the nanocomposite and coating were examined using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, TESCAN MIRA3) with a working voltage of 30 kV. The absorption spectrum of the nanocomposite was recorded using a Uv-Vis spectrometer (Thermo Company) in the wavelength range between 200 and 800 nm.

S. aureus ATCC 33591 and E. coli 10536 (Iranian Biological Resource Center) were tested for antimicrobial sensitivity of CMC/CuO NPs/CQDs susceptibility by the modified Kirby-Bauer test [25]. Muller Hinton (MH) agar was swabbed to give uniform growth with 0.5 McFarland (1.5 × 108 CFU/ml) inoculation. The disk diffusion was done using the commercial disk of gentamicin 10 μg (GEN) as positive control and the blank discs (6 mm in diameter) impregnated with 20 μl of CMC/CuO NPs/CQDs 0 mg/ml (Distilled water), 2.5 mg/ml, and 10 mg/ml, respectively. Subsequently, the plates were placed in an incubator set at a temperature of 37 °C for 24 h. Following the incubation period, the diameters of the growth inhibition zones were assessed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Minimum inhibitory concentration and minimum bactericidal concentration determination
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC), from the stock of CMC/CuO NPs/CQDs (50 mg/ml), five dilutions were prepared to twofold serial dilutions using 1000 μl of MH broth and incubated with 1000 μl of 0.5 MacFarland standard bacterial cultures (S. aureus or E. coli) inoculum for 24 h at 37 °C. For negative controls, the microtubes were without the CMC/CuO NPs/ CQDs and bacteria, respectively. Eventually, 20 μl of each sample was streaked onto the MH agar plates and incubated overnight at 37 °C. MIC was determined as the lowest dose of CMC/CuO NPs/CQDs at which bacterial colonies were observed. Minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) was considered the dose of nanocomposite that inhibited the growth of bacteria [26].

ISO standard 10993-5 was used for investigation of Cytotoxicity of nanocomposite. The preparation of extracts was done according to ISO standard 10993-12 [27]. The NIH/3T3 mouse embryonic fibroblast cell line (ATCC CRL-1658, USA) served as the chosen cellular model in this work. The cells were initially placed at a concentration of 1 × 105 cell/ml in order to pre-incubate in the micro-titration test plates (TPP, Switzerland) for a duration of 24 h. Afterwards, the cultivation medium was substituted with a pre-prepared blend of culture media containing extracts and incubated for a duration of 24 h. Then, the cell viability was determined by MTT assay [28].

By calculation of malondialdehyde (MDA) concentration, the level of lipid oxidation for CQDs in nanocomposite was detected. For this purpose, the mixtures of nanocomposite (including CQDs with concentration of 0, 25, 50, 75 and 100 μg/ml) and E. coli and cultured were centrifuged at 5 °C and 3000 rpm for 10 min for 24 h, and then washed with solution of PBS for three times. Next, 1.5 ml of MDA extract was introduced. Subsequently, the solutions underwent ultrasonic decomposition for a duration of 15 min, followed by centrifugation at 5 °C and 5000 rpm for 15 min to gather the supernatant. After that, 0.2 ml of samples were mixed with 0.2 ml HCl (0.5 mol/l) and 0.33 ml TBA reagent (0.67 %), and heated at 90 °C for 25 min. After the samples had been cooled down to ambient temperature, 3.0 ml of methanol was introduced, followed by shaking the solution for a duration of 40 min. Finally, the measurement of the absorbance at 534 nm was conducted on the collected upper liquid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of changes of bacterial morphology
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of bacteria before and after of treated with nanocomposite was investigated by using SEM microscopy. For this purpose, 100 μL of solutions of treated E. coli and S. aureus bacteria were added to 900 μL of 2.5 % solution of glutaraldehyde, separately, and maintained for 10 h in 4 °C. Then, the solutions treated with ethanol for dehydration for 15 min. After, 10 μL of the solution of bacterial was dropped onto the wafer of silicon and dried for 24 h in an oven at 70 °C. Finally, the silicon wafers examined using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, TESCAN MIRA3) with a working voltage of 30 kV.

Te TEM analysis was conducted to examine the morphology and size distribution of carbon quantum dots. The obtained results are presented in Fig. 1 (a and b), respectively. The results showed that CQDs have a spherical morphology and narrow size distribution and well dispersed. The synthesized CQDs have a size between 2 and 10 nm with a mean size of ~5.6 nm.

The X-ray diffraction technique was employed to examine the crystal structure and phases of both the nanocomposite and its constituent. The findings, depicted in Fig. 2 , reveal that the X-ray diffraction pattern of copper oxide nanoparticles closely resembles the standard data (JCPDS 1268-80). Miller indices (h k l), (−110), (002), (111), (−202), (020), (202), (−113), (−311), (113), (311) and (004) respectively in the angles of 2θ is equal to 32.35, 35.34, 38.86, 48.75, 53.41, 56.38, 61.47, 66.10, 68/86, 71/62, 78/86° was observed, which confirms the formation of copper oxide monoclinic phase [29–32]. In the X-ray diffraction pattern of carboxymethyl cellulose, a distinct peak was observed at 2θ = 21.62°, which is due to the formation of intramolecular hydronic bonds in the presence of free amino groups in carboxymethyl cellulose [29–31]. The X-ray diffraction pattern of the carbon dot shows a distinct peak in 2θ = 20/62° that is related to the Miller index of (002) [33]. The X-ray diffraction pattern of CMC/CuO NPs nanocomposite clearly shows the peaks related to copper oxide nanoparticles at angles of 2θ = 35.34°, 38.86° and 48.75°. Also, the peak related to carboxymethyl cellulose with weakened intensity can also be seen. As it is shown in the X-ray diffraction pattern of CMC/CuO NPs/CQDs nanocomposite, the peaks of copper oxide nanoparticles at the angles of 2θ = 35/34°, 38/86°, 48/75°, 61/62°, and 68/86° are seen in this diffraction pattern. The intensity of these peaks has decreased to some extent due to the interaction with other components and also with the polymer matrix. The very weak peak observed at about 2θ = 21° degrees is related to the carbon quantum dot and carboxymethyl cellulose, which is greatly weakened due to the overlapping of these peaks. X-ray diffraction results confirm the presence of all components in the final nanocomposite.

Fig. 3 displays the FTIR spectrum of the nanocomposite and its individual components. In all samples, an observable broad peak at approximately 3414 cm−1 indicates the presence of symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of the OH group. Also, the peaks corresponding to CuOCu stretching vibration were observed at 531 and 582 cm−1 in the IR spectrum of copper oxide nanoparticles [29,31,32,34,35].

The stretching vibration of the CH group is indicated by a peak at 2921 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of carboxymethyl cellulose. Additionally, the peak at 1617 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the COO group. Also, the peak observed at 1421 cm−1 is related to the bending vibration of the COONa group. The peaks observed at 1328 cm−1 and 1059 cm−1 were respectively related to the stretching vibration of CH2 and CHOCH groups [29,31,36].

The stretching vibration of the CH2 and COOH groups can be attributed to the observed peaks at 3236 cm−1 and 1707 cm−1, respectively, in the FTIR spectrum of carbon quantum dot. Similarly, the bending vibration of the NH2 group and the stretching vibration of the CC group are responsible for the peaks observed at 1617 cm−1 and 1401 cm−1, respectively. Additionally, a peak at 1183 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the CO group. Lastly, the bending vibration of the CH group is represented by peaks at 777 cm−1 and 624 cm−1 [33].

The peak related to the stretching vibration of the CHOCH group was observed in the range of 1059 cm−1 in the IR spectrum of two-component and three-component nanocomposites. Also, the peaks related to CuOCu stretching vibration for nanocomposites were observed at 531 and 582 cm−1. The peak observed at 1617 cm−1 in the three-component nanocomposite spectrum is related to the bending vibration of the NH2 group of carbon quantum dot. The results of the IR spectrum of nanocomposite confirm the presence of copper oxide nanoparticles and carbon quantum dot in the polymer matrix and indicates the formation of nanocomposite.

The UV–Vis spectroscopy results of the final nanocomposite and its components are shown in Fig. 4 . As can be seen, pure carboxymethyl cellulose has a relatively high transmittance in both the UV and visible regions and therefore does not show any distinct absorption bands in its UV–Vis spectrum [37,38]. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of copper oxide nanoparticles shows a broad absorption band with a wavelength of 285 nm. The carbon quantum dot's absorption spectrum exhibits two absorption peaks at 226 and 280 nm wavelengths. These peaks correspond to the π − π* transition of the CC group and the n − π* transition of the CO group, respectively [39]. The absorption spectrum of the nanocomposite shows two characteristic peaks at 225 and 282 nm, the first of which corresponds to the peak of the carbon quantum dot and the second can be attributed to both the carbon quantum dot and copper oxide nanoparticles (because the absorption peaks of carbon quantum dot and copper oxide nanoparticles overlap in this range). The nanocomposite's absorbance is influenced by several factors, encompassing intrinsic material properties like particle size and refractive index of the components, as well as fabrication factors such as nanocomposite thickness, filler concentration, surface roughness, and particle dispersion state. Consequently, particles exceeding the visible wavelength in size would impede light, resulting in films that are either translucent or opaque [40]. The results of the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the nanocomposite confirm the presence of copper oxide nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots in the polymer matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FESEM micrograph and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 (a, b) displays the field emission scanning electron microscope images of the nanocomposite at two distinct magnifications. In these images, the nanocomposite is in the form of large and compact surface structures, which are most likely the polymer layers that surround CuO NPs and CQDs. The establishment of such a framework structure is probably an outcome of the increase in the expansion rate of the polymer matrix when copper oxide nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots are incorporated [41–43]. The histogram of nanocomposite size distribution (Fig. 5c) shows that the size of these compact surface structures is distributed in the range of 40–160 nm. Fig. 5d shows the results of X-ray energy diffraction spectroscopy of a random spot on these large surface structures. Elemental analysis shows the presence of carbon, oxygen, and copper elements with weight percentages of 41.18, 50.37, and 8.45, respectively, which indicates the presence of all nanocomposite components and confirms its formation.

The examination of the coating's quality, microstructure, and the presence of surface defects like microcracks was conducted using field emission scanning electron microscopy. The findings are illustrated in Fig. 6 . As shown in Fig. 6a and b, the nanocomposite coating has an almost uniform and homogeneous structure at all points, which indicates the proper distribution of the nanocomposite components on the substrate. The white heterogeneous deposit observed in Fig. 6b (whose surface morphology is shown in higher magnification in Fig. 6c) is caused by the local non-uniform dispersion and agglomeration of the polymer matrix in the coating. This localized deposition, due to the creation of residual stress, has led to the creation of microcracks around it, which can be seen in the image. The cross-section image of the coating is shown in Fig. 6e and f. As shown in the pictures, the coating thickness is almost uniform in the entire cross-section of the coating layer and is between 2.7 and 2.9 μm.

To investigate the distribution of copper oxide nanoparticles and carbon quantum dot in the polymer matrix of the coating, linear X-ray energy diffraction analysis was performed on backscattered electron image of the coating (Fig. 6d), and the results and weight percentage changes of the elements along the specified line for 19 points (p1) to (p19) are given in Fig. 7 and Table 2 . The findings indicate that all elements are present in the coating, with varying weight ratios at each point. According to the weight percentages corresponding to these 19 points in Table 2, the lowest and highest copper weight percentages belong to points P12 and P17, which have 2.36 and 25.67 wt%, respectively, and the other points have weight percentages between these minimal and maximum values. The non-uniform distribution of elemental weight in the coating is a result of the high specific surface area of nanoparticles and their tendency to cluster together in certain areas [44].

In order to confirm the presence of CuO NPs and CQDS in the polymer matrix and to check their dispersion distribution in the nanocomposite and coating, elemental mapping analysis was performed, and the results are shown in Fig. 8 . The results show a good dispersion of copper, oxygen and carbon elements with minimal agglomeration in the matrix of polymer nanocomposite and coating. The results of element distribution observed by elemental mapping analysis are consistent with the weight percentage distribution results calculated by linear energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy of coatings (Fig. 7).

The findings revealed no inhibition growth zone around the blank disk and CMC/CuO NPs/CQDs 10 mg/ml disk as indicated in Fig. 9 (A and B). Moreover, the nanocomposite at 2.5 mg/ml only inhibited the growth of S. aureus and its inhibition zone length was 3 mm. On the other hand, the inhibition zones for E. coli and S. aureus were 17 mm and 32 mm, respectively, at 10 μg/disc of gentamicin. (Fig. 9, A and B). It is imperative to note that the Kirby-Bauer method heavily relies on the diffusivity of the experimental substances. Nanoparticles larger than 10 nm have a diffusivity 10 times lower than antibiotics, hence they cannot interact with bacteria in agar [45]. Also, diffusion is more related to the movement of ions instead of nanoparticles [46,47]. It's possible that the disc diffusion method is not accurate for observing the nanoparticle's antibacterial performance, due to the size of the nanoparticles in this study (50 nm) and the non-ionizable composition. Hence, it may lead to a decreased diffusion of nanoparticles within the culture medium and hinder their interaction with bacteria [45,48,49]. It seems the high concentration of nanocomposites affects their agglomeration [50] which may also explain why nanocomposite 2.5 mg/ml compared to 10 mg/ml had an inhibition growth zone in the disk diffusion test.

According to the results, the CMC/CuO NPs/CQDs was effective in the MIC assays against S. aureus and E. coli with MIC values of 25 mg/ml and >50 mg/ml, respectively (Figs. 10B and 11B ). The study found that the nanocomposite showed bactericidal properties against S. aureus at a concentration of 50 mg/ml, whereas even the highest tested concentrations of CMC/CuO NPs/CQDs (>50 mg/ml) were not effective against E. coli (as shown in Figs. 10A and 11A). The variances in the bacterial cell wall and its membrane components could potentially account for these discoveries. At a neutral pH, the bacterial cells, regardless of whether they are gram-negative or gram-positive, exhibit an overall negative charge. This is primarily attributed to the existence of peptidoglycan (carboxyl and amino groups) and teichoic acids (phosphate-rich components). On the other hand, metal-based nanoparticles carry a net positive charge. This allows NPs to attach to the microbial cell wall and cell membrane, disrupting the bacterial cell wall and increasing its permeability [51]. Furthermore, upon binding to the bacterial membrane, copper has the ability to induce depolarization of the bacterial cell membrane. This, in turn, leads to a decrease in the potential difference across the membrane. As a result, NPs can easily penetrate the cell and cause bacterial death [52].

Our observations show that the bactericidal effect of CMC/CuO NPs/CQDs nanocomposite on S. aureus is stronger than that of E. coli. A possible reason for this phenomenon is that gram-positive bacteria possess a greater quantity of peptidoglycan and proteins compared to gram-negative bacteria [53,54]. However, proteins exhibit a greater binding affinity for copper [55]. Therefore, S. aureus as a gram-positive bacteria, which contains more peptidoglycan and proteins is more susceptible to copper compared to E. coli as a gram-negative bacteria. Besides, Sharan's research demonstrated that copper is more effective at inactivating E. coli at pH 6 and 9 compared to pH 7, as well as high temperatures [56].

The investigation of the cytotoxic property of the CMC/CuO NPs/CQDs nanocomposite is of utmost importance, as it has potential applications as an antibacterial surface. The cytotoxicity of the nanocomposite was assessed using the MTT assay and results are shown in supporting information (SI), Fig. S1. The testing methodology adheres to the ISO standard, wherein the cells are cultured using either a pure cultivation medium or a cultivation medium blended with different extract concentrations derived from the tested samples. The NIH/3 T3 cell line served as the standard cell line utilized for cytotoxicity analysis. It is evident that nanocomposites exhibit no cytotoxicity, suggesting their potential application as a protective coating for medical devices during brief interactions with the human body.

The results of ROS generation are shown in SI, Fig. S2. As can be seen, the MDA levels showed a steady increase as the concentration of CQDs in nanocomposite increased, pointing towards lipid peroxidation in bavteria caused by CQDs. The amount of % ROS production with the increase in the concentration of CQDs is very low, also the % ROS production for E.coli and S. aureus is almost the same, which is the reason for not using a photostimulator [57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The morphology changes of the bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: The bacteria morphology is investigated by SEM to evaluate the native and the treated cells (see SI, Fig. S3). As seen in Fig. S3a and c, the original bacterial cells display a typical shape with an undamaged membrane envelope, smooth surface, and absence of adhesion phenomenon, in contrast to the treated cells with nanocomposite, showing rougher surface and shrinkage (Fig. S3b and S3d). Cells exhibit notable morphological variances, suggesting alterations in the permeability of their membranes. The damage on the membrane of cell prevents the regulation of transmembrane transport, ultimately leading to the death of the cell.

In this research, a novel antibacterial bionanocomposite based on carboxymethyl cellulose polymer/copper oxide nanoparticles/carbon quantum dots was prepared using the direct solution method and coated on medical grade 316 stainless steel by sol-gel dip-coating method. The results showed that CQDs have spherical morphology, well dispersed, and size between 2 and 10 nm with a mean size of ~5.6 nm. Various characterization methods confirmed the desirable structural properties of both the studied bionanocomposite and its components. The coating analysis showed that copper oxide nanoparticles and carbon quantum dot are uniformly distributed in the polymer matrix and create a uniform coating on the substrate. The results show that the bactericidal effect of CMC/CuO NPs/CQDs on S. aureus is stronger than that of E. coli. The results of MIC and MBC assay show the CMC/CuO NPs/CQDs was effective in the MIC assays against S. aureus and E. coli with MIC values of 25 mg/ml and >50 mg/ml, respectively. On the other hand, the results of the disk diffusion assay show the inhibition zones for E. coli and S. aureus were 17 mm and 32 mm, respectively, at 10 μg/disc of gentamicin. Due to the advantageous structural and antibacterial characteristics possessed by the CMC/CuO NPs/CQDs bionanocomposite, it can serve as a highly efficient antibacterial agent.

Hamidreza Amoon: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Data curation. Ayoub Moghadam: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Maryam Chalabi Hajkarim: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2024.131922.
